Pre-Operations:

Drive 1 has clean Ubuntu LTS 24.03 image from the first time ever installing Ubuntu. We can use this to return to stable OS and migrate from there. I gave up on Ubuntu as I had too many headaches with drivers. I decided to migrate to Mint OS as I learned through research that this distro is more supported for NVIDIA GPUs and network drivers. For this to work, I needed a working OS to be able to flash Mint OS to drive 2\. 

To easily document this without using local storage (a hassle with data transfer on multiple USB drives), I used Google Docs to quickly write my notes as I went and insert screenshots, making good use of cloud storage.

I then transferred all files from drive to drive 3 as this one has more storage and storage is a commodity today. 

Steps:

- [ ] ~~Back Up important files to drive 3~~  
- [ ] ~~Remove drive 3~~  
- [x] ~~Insert drive 1~~  
- [x] ~~Reboot into BIOS~~  
- [x] ~~Reinstall Ubuntu from drive 1~~  
- [x] ~~Download Mint Linux~~  
- [x] ~~Flash Mint to drive 2~~  
- [x] ~~Install Mint from drive 2~~

Tools:

Drive 1 had 16 GB \- SanDisk  
Drive 2 has PNK \- 16 GB  
Drive 3 has 2 TB \- ext hard drive by WD  
balenaEtcher  
Linux Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS  
Linux Mint OS  
Google Docs

Conclusion:

Drive 1 holds IT Tools  
Drive 2 holds Mint image  
Drive 3 holds backup

Project Notes:

The idea essentially is to switch the formatting of drive 1 and drive 2, transferring the IT Tools in drive 2 to drive 1, then flashing Mint OS to drive 2 and using this for migration. 

I tried to backup my files before starting, but the drivers were completely broken. Could not get my external hard drive to mount. 

Could not backup files as exFAT was not being loaded by the kernel. This was a total failure of an update. Most important folder scripts was backed up to GitHub. From my days working in digital arts, I am accustomed to keeping all important files on an external hard drive, intended for this purpose alone. This saved my ass. Whatever was lost was the least important files of notes and drafts that I would not miss.   
![][image1]  
Installed Ubuntu again first to be able to flash Mint OS to drive 2\.    
![][image2]  
Ubuntu installed successfully. 

**Partition Drives**  
![][image3]  
SanDisk has 2 partitions, created for future reinstalls. The first partition is the flashed ubuntu image, the second is the writable logs directory. 

![][image4]  
![][image5]  
The first Ubuntu install was done by flashing the image to the USB drive using [balenaEtcher](https://etcher.balena.io/).

To format SanDisk  
We essentially just want to repeat this same process by switching the flash drives available. We will be formatting drive 1 back into a standard storage drive. Then, we will format drive 2 and flash Mint here with balenaEtcher.

Ubuntu's image format (based on a system called **casper**) automatically creates that writable partition. According to Claude, Mint OS does not create a writable partition like Ubuntu does. If we need persistence on a Mint live USB, we will need to use a tool like **Ventoy** or **mkusb** to set that up manually. For this installation, this is not a necessary step but thought it’d be worth mentioning. 

Brand New USB \- PNY 16 GB Formatted   
![][image6]  
Erase saves time here as the flash drive is new  
![][image7]  
Ready for use  
![][image8]  
![][image9]  
![][image10]  
Since I am looking for the most support, I went with Cinnamon, the most popular edition.   
![][image11]Clicking Download brings you here.

![][image12]You need to choose a “mirror.” A quick search on Reddit indicated to download the mirror closest to you globally, but it essentially does not matter. 

![][image13]  
I went with the one I liked the name the most: “Linux Freedom.”

![][image14]  
Moved Mint OS from Downloads to new USB flash drive.

![][image15]  
![][image16]  
Installed correct Linux x64 version for my system architecture.  
![][image17]  
Unzip in downloads  
![][image18]  
![][image19]

![][image20]  
![][image21]  
On Etcher

Right click \> Run (or Enter)

1. **Flash from file** → navigate to the USB and select the `mint.iso`  
2. **Select target** → pick that same USB drive  
3. **Flash\!**

Etcher will handle the rest — it'll wipe the Ext4 format, write the ISO, and verify it automatically. The drive will come out the other end as a bootable Linux Mint installer.

![][image22]  
Finds flash drive instantly  
![][image23]

![][image24]  
![][image25]  
![][image26]

Restart \> Boot into BIOS \> Select flash as boot device.   
Followed installation process from Mint, removed USB device and successfully installed the OS.  
![][image27]
